headline: Montana Goes Dry: Statewide Prohibition Begins, 1918
body:
The clock hands crept towards midnight on December 31, 1918, not merely marking the passage of one year into the next, but signaling a profound and immediate transformation across the vast expanse of Montana. This was no ordinary New Year’s Eve; it was the eve of a forced sobriety, a statewide experiment in moral engineering. A full year before the nation grappled with its Eighteenth Amendment, Montana, by popular vote in 1916, had already cast its lot with the Drys. The implications, as the final hours of legal liquor ticked away, were stark and immediate, echoing through every saloon, every home, and every pocketbook in the state.

The Great Falls Tribune, ever the chronicler of the common man’s fate, captured the mood with a headline that rang with both finality and a touch of grim humor: “Good-Bye Booze.” The article, published on December 30, 1918, laid bare the uncompromising nature of the new law. “This is the last day you can legally purchase a drink of anything capable of being used as a beverage which contains alcohol in any quantity, or give it away unless you are a minister of the gospel of Christ’s religion and give it at a communion service.” Even the innocent lemon or vanilla extract, staples of the pantry, fell under the ban. The prudent, those who had perhaps secreted away a private hoard, might have felt a fleeting sense of security, but the prohibitionists, zealous in their victory, were already whispering of amendments to seize and confiscate such hidden reserves. It was a war declared not just on drink, but on the very habit of it, on the culture that had grown around it, and on the personal liberties that had, until then, been taken for granted.

The economic tremors were immediate and undeniable. Great Falls alone braced for a loss of approximately $60,000 in annual revenue, a sum that, in 1918, represented a significant blow to county coffers. The Drys had promised that this deficit would be offset by a dramatic reduction in police, jail, and court costs, a vision of a crime-free, orderly society born from enforced abstinence. Yet, the Wets, those who had argued for moderation and warned of job losses, saw a different future. They foresaw a landscape dotted with “blind pigs,” illicit establishments where the law would be flouted, and a new breed of criminal, the bootlegger, would thrive. The Tribune, with a cynical eye, doubted the efficacy of such underground operations, noting, “A pig is not a silent animal, and a blind pig cannot be kept in city or country very long without squeals that betray its locality.” The peace officers, it was reported, were fully determined to enforce the law strictly, making it “unhealthy for blind pigs or bootleggers.”

As the final hours of December 31st dwindled, the atmosphere in places like Great Falls was a curious blend of revelry and resignation. The Great Falls Tribune, in its December 31st edition, painted a vivid picture of the transition under the headline “Barelycorn Takes Count at Midnight.” It described a “jollification, but Little Rowdyism, Marks Close of Old Regime in Great Falls on Eve of Inauguration of Prohibition.” The city, it seemed, took the death of “John Barleycorn” philosophically, with a wake that many believed would never be seen again. Even those who had voted for prohibition joined in the good-natured farewell. There were but ten arrests for drunkenness, a figure far from a record, indicating a surprisingly subdued end to an era. The article quoted Omar Khayyam, a poignant reflection on the fleeting nature of pleasure: “The bird of Time has but a little way to flutter – and the bird is on the wing.”

Saloon owners, facing the abrupt end of their livelihoods, made their final sales. George E. Edman, proprietor of the White House bar, found himself serving only wine by 10 o’clock, while Frank Dalton of the Palace bar had sold out all but two bottles of champagne, with only whisky and a little beer remaining. Edman, a saloonman for thirty years, reflected on his past, his voice tinged with trepidation as he spoke to a reporter. “All my patrons are on their best behavior,” he remarked, “Although they never felt better in their lives. Say now, remember that I am a man of family.” Across the street, the Stockholm bar, having sold out all its wet goods and even its fixtures by 9 o’clock, stood completely extinguished. Dalton, ever the pragmatist, planned to transform his Palace into a poolroom, offering soft drinks and confections. The lights in the barrooms flickered low, a symbolic dimming of an age. As one weary bystander quipped, “the juice at the powerhouse has gone dry.” The first day of 1919, under the new dry rule, saw a stark contrast to the previous year: not a single case of inebriety was recorded on the police blotter, a testament to the immediate, if superficial, impact of the law. The grand experiment had begun, its long-term consequences yet to unfold across the rugged landscape of Montana.
